item.title: Night Patrol, 2025 - ★½
item.description: When I declared it Justin Long January earlier this year, I’d had meant for this to be the cake topper so to speak. Unfortunately it was so poor in nearly every facet that I forgot to finish, then forgot to log so I’m doing that now. Major whiff, Justin Long January has been postponed indefinitely and I’m taking no questions at this time.
item.dc:creator: Jacob H

item.title: Happiness, 1998 - ★★★★★
item.description:
I don’t even know where to begin with this because it feels like every character is operating at a constant baseline of self-hatred and the movie just refuses to let any of them off the hook, it keeps drifting from person to person like nobody deserves to be centered and somehow that makes it worse, and it’ll just drop into the most deeply uncomfortable places without warning and move on like it’s nothing, no emphasis, just this steady accumulation of everything you don’t want to sit with, and the fact that it’s genuinely funny on top of all that almost feels hostile because you catch yourself laughing and immediately feel gross about it, like it’s exposing something in you as much as it is in them, and I hate how much I actually enjoyed it because by the end you feel completely drained and a little sick. With a relentless film that goes for some of the sequences and jokes that are so unbelievably, yet maybe the funniest is now having a crisp 4K version of this that now sits on my shelf forever for everyone to see.

Elite first date watch or maybe first holiday with new in-laws.
item.dc:creator: Jacob H

item.title: Full Metal Jacket, 1987 - ★★★★½
item.description: Crazy that my Dad had me watch this and The Shining before the age of 10. Feels so deliberate with every choice, especially the longer that time has passed.
item.dc:creator: Jacob H

item.title: Avatar: Fire and Ash, 2025 - ★★
item.description: Watched on Monday March 30, 2026.
item.dc:creator: Jacob H

item.title: The Gold Rush, 1925 - ★★★★
item.description: 1925 original cut instead of the 1942 rerelease. It was said that this has no narration, is longer, and less smoothed out in aspects. It does feel scrappier because of that. The jokes kind of sneak up on you and some of it is honestly a little bleak in an odd way. Really enjoyed a few of the more serious moments in the back half. I don’t know if it’s as clean as City Lights, but I like how you’re finding it as it goes. Otherworldly impressive given the circumstances.
item.dc:creator: Jacob H

item.title: True Romance, 1993 - ★★½
item.description:
Tony Scott the absolute legend saves this with his operatic violence, sweltering romance conjured out of thin air against some pretty hokey dialogue and characters if I’m being honest. Really apparent who penned this script and what sort of self-insert fantasy this is, hard to not find it distracting. Kind of thought the best part about this was when they showed 20 seconds of the final shootout of A Better Tomorrow II but what do I know.

The movie only threatens to work at all, when Christian Slater isn’t talking or on the screen. I said what I said.
item.dc:creator: Jacob H

item.title: Nirvanna the Band the Show the Movie, 2025 - ★★★★½
item.letterboxd:filmTitle: Nirvanna the Band the Show the Movie
item.description: Watched on Saturday March 28, 2026.
item.dc:creator: Jacob H

item.title: You Were Never Really Here, 2017 - ★★★★½
item.description:
It doesn’t really unfold so much as flicker in and out. Feels like fragments of a broken life more than a story, pieces that don’t want to sit still long enough for you to grab onto them. You’re effectively playing catch up in a way that’s consistently nerve wracking. More than the obvious comparisons to Taken and Taxi Driver, I kept thinking about The Limey because of the narrative destruction. It’s that same kind of story underneath, but completely hollowed out and rearranged. An insane concoction that does end up checking all my boxes.

The score from Jonny Greenwood is insane, just this constant pressure sitting on your chest. Flips between tranquility and more like wanting to trap you in the same headspace which is concerning to say the least. Everything feels off, like it could slip permanently at any second. And it never does the thing where it pulls the rug out from under you. It’s worse than that. It just keeps moving, like a carpet ride you can’t step off of, whether you’re on board or not. Feels like there was a longer version of this at one point, more connective tissue that got stripped away. But even at this quietly coiled, death-spring 85 minutes, it gets almost everything across. Moody destabilization the whole way through, outstanding film and Joaquin is phenomenal to the surprise of nobody.
item.dc:creator: Jacob H

item.title: L.A. Confidential, 1997 - ★★★★
item.description:
There’s something kind of sneaky about how L.A. Confidential works so seamlessly on the viewer. It feels classical right away, like it’s settling into the language of those 40s and 50s noirs, taking its time, letting the camera establish a space before it really starts to squeeze anything out of it. Then you realize how controlled all of that patience is, how every room is being set up to trap someone in it. The way it uses reflections really stuck with me this time. Glass, mirrors or windows of any kind, people constantly framed through something else. It’s not just a stylistic choice since it starts to feel like the whole movie is refusing to give you a clean image of anything. Everyone is being split, doubled, distorted somewhat, which lines up with how information moves through the story. Nothing arrives straight.

It feels in direct conversation with the old noirs, but not in a dress-up way. It actually understands what made those movies tick, structurally and morally, and then just runs that system with a more modern sense of cause and effect. Chinatown is the obvious comparison point, but that one feels heavier, more openly fatalistic. This is smoother, almost deceptively so. It moves like a clean cut procedural but underneath it is something just as sickly uncontrollable. As a bridge, it carries over that old Hollywood sense of clarity, where everything is legible and watchable and constantly in motion, but it’s plugged into something much more cynical. You’re getting the myth and the autopsy at the same time without the movie ever announcing it. And it’s so easy to watch, almost suspiciously easy. Characters slide into place, information keeps turning over, and before you really stop to question anything you’re already deep in it.

I like how that plays out through Guy Pearce’s character especially. When is he going to get his flowers because he’s just exceptional in every role. He starts off feeling almost like a joke, getting clowned for the glasses, completely unsure of himself, clearly not built for the room he’s been placed in. But the movie never breaks its own logic for him. He grows into the job in a way that feels earned, even when it turns violent, and by the time he’s standing there fresh-faced after doing something brutal, it doesn’t even feel like a contradiction anymore. It just feels like the system working exactly as intended. Long overdue but glad I finally got to knock it off my watch list.
item.dc:creator: Jacob H

item.title: Peaky Blinders: The Immortal Man, 2026 - ★★½
item.description:
I hope Cillian got a dump truck full a gold coins for this. By golly they be milking every last drop out of something. Hypothetically they can just roll out an episode and a half of any show and call it an extension or spin off so hard not to feel a certain way. Plus it’s just a whole lot of “Ders a roight proper war goin on in me mind, innit. Thoughts scrappin about like they’re down a the pub, no order to it mate, chaos in me mental lad.”

Wasn’t a big fan of every moody alternative cover but wasn’t going to say anything until they butchered Massive Attack like they did, despicable act. Kind of what I expected considering the show never really grabbed me at any point. Perhaps I wish it wasn’t directed by the same person who made Heart of Stone most recently but it seems to be working out all the same.
item.dc:creator: Jacob H

item.title: Code of Silence, 1985 - ★★★
item.description:
Meant to honor Chuck Norris only to realize that I’ve never seen a film of his in its entirety, somehow. I’ve watched so much Walker, Texas Ranger growing up so it seems unlikely to have not seen any but it’s true. Not only does Code of Silence use Chicago to a tremendous ability, they also give me Dennis Farina and Henry Silva. Andrew Davis at the helm so yeah this is being elevated pretty consistently, looks way better and is much louder than you’d anticipate with some well executed action sequences.

Chuck Norris gets an M72 LAW before the final confrontation. Deliciously excessive in the right spots so I’d watch this again in a heartbeat.
item.dc:creator: Jacob H

item.title: Highway Patrolman, 1991 - ★★★★
item.description: Watched on Tuesday March 24, 2026.
item.dc:creator: Jacob H

item.title: Nirvanna the Band the Show the Movie, 2025 - ★★★★½
item.letterboxd:filmTitle: Nirvanna the Band the Show the Movie
item.description:
I missed it in theaters and I keep thinking about what it would’ve been like to see it with a crowd. “I really hope your life turned out the way you wanted”. Maybe the best movie I’ve ever seen about real enduring friendship. Will absolutely be one of my favorites all year and am seated for whatever Matt Johnson does going forward. Genius lunatic stuff no doubt.

2026 Ranked
item.dc:creator: Jacob H

item.title: Hunger, 2008 - ★★★★
item.description:
I still vividly remember watching Shame a few years ago and being so floored with the examination of Fassbender’s addicted, tortured character. Despite thinking constantly about it, I’ve hesitated pulling a rewatch because of how intense it is. Like made with elite craftsmanship all throughout yet genuinely uncomfortable in its authenticity.

Well I pulled the trigger on the earlier collaboration between McQueen and Fassbender, just as upsetting, perhaps more. Equally as impressed because of how much is accomplished in such a runtime, you can clearly see a consummate professional at the helm. The prison drama element is pretty as expected, especially with the true story element however there are many moments that are supremely elevated by the direction. Acting is on point from everyone given the intensity. Liam Cunningham and Michael Fassbender have some great chemistry once they both finally show up.

Decidedly unfun and something pivotal to wash over me.

Sad, Bitter, Angry
item.dc:creator: Jacob H

item.title: To Live and Die in L.A., 1985 - ★★★★★
item.letterboxd:filmTitle: To Live and Die in L.A.
item.description: Watched on Monday March 23, 2026.
item.dc:creator: Jacob H

item.title: Wuthering Heights, 2026 - ★★
item.description:
I’m a mild Saltburn defender and I’m coming to this without any real attachment to the source material, but this just feels like the wrong instinct entirely. Wuthering Heights gets turned into something oddly safe. Certain choices are loud, but not for any real purpose. It all leans into that hyper-curated, music video kind of aesthetic where every color and composition is noticeable but nothing is being said out of it. The whole thing just sits there as a standard period romance tragedy. There’s no sense that these people are being eaten alive by their environment or each other. Of all the directions you could take this story, it is curious as to why this was something Fennell felt she had to put on screen.

2026 Ranked
item.dc:creator: Jacob H

item.title: Balls of Fury, 2007 - ★★
item.description: Watched on Monday March 23, 2026.
item.dc:creator: Jacob H

item.title: Red Dawn, 1984 - ★★½
item.description:
Watching this now is almost surreal. The Soviet/Cuban invaders speak in this weirdly persuasive critique of capitalism and the teen resistance fighting for freedom ends up looking almost absurd. There is tension. Harry Dean Stanton shows up for a few minutes as a father on the edge carrying the weight of the story but PG-13 keeps it all at arm’s length. Violence is suggested, stakes feel abstract and the desperation never fully lands.

Compared to something like Blue Thunder which is paranoid and unhinged in a way that feels immediate and dangerous, Red Dawn is a sanitized Cold War fantasy. Still there is something compelling in those flashes of real danger and in how the invaders’ ideology now reads uncomfortably sharp. Last 30 minutes is good but the runtime should have been around 97 minutes. I’ve also definitely seen pretty much this whole thing before.
item.dc:creator: Jacob H

item.title: Mars Attacks!, 1996
item.description: Watched on Saturday March 21, 2026.
item.dc:creator: Jacob H

item.title: The Departed, 2006 - ★★★★
item.description: Watched on Friday March 20, 2026.
item.dc:creator: Jacob H

item.title: Heat, 1995 - ★★★★★
item.description:
Another trip around the sun, you could say this was a little gift for me. A short list of things I can always count on, this absolute masterpiece is certainly near the top of it. Still makes my soul flutter countless times and it doesn’t matter how many times I see it.


“I had to get it on, he was making a move, I had to get it on.”
item.dc:creator: Jacob H

item.title: Dragged Across Concrete, 2018 - ★★★★½
item.description: Watched on Thursday March 19, 2026.
item.dc:creator: Jacob H

item.title: Whistle, 2025 - ★★
item.description:
As far as gateway horror goes, you can do worse. Thought it reminded me of something like The Nun or Annabelle then I saw Corin Hardy directed so everything checks out.

2026 Ranked
item.dc:creator: Jacob H

item.title: End of Watch, 2012 - ★★★½
item.description: One of the many deeply evil movies that I do find myself enjoying unfortunately. David Ayer is a dumpster diving expert, no question about that.
item.dc:creator: Jacob H

item.title: The Moment, 2026 - ★★★
item.description:
It opens with aggressive noise and release play, especially with the Gaspar Noé graphics but pulls back almost immediately. From there it stays quieter, more controlled, more self aware than expected. I kept waiting for it to swing back into something bigger and it never really does. A lot of it plays like someone who understands exactly what this is supposed to be and keeps adjusting in real time. The ideas stack, the tone shifts, never really spreads out at all and stays compact until the last seconds. Then at the end it finally just says it which I wish didn’t happen.

Also really wanted more music, more lights and the works. Starts strong and has a warning but it dials it back pretty quickly.

2026 Ranked
item.dc:creator: Jacob H

item.title: Return to Silent Hill, 2026 - ★
item.description: I mean….we are one step away from having video game adaptations just be you going to the theater to play the game. Looks maybe how they wanted it to, personally thought it was painful to the eyes. Nobody hates the Silent Hill IP more than Christophe Gans.
item.dc:creator: Jacob H

item.title: Gladiator, 2000 - ★★★★½
item.description:
Been watching this my whole life, usually with my Dad and I finally got to see it with him on the big screen. I mean this was such a reach back into the time capsule from Ridley back in 2000, it’s only more of an anomaly as time has passed. Capital M-Motion Picture. The performances are everything you want out of the biggest films being made, complete extravagance is being met with real gravitas from everyone, regardless of what’s being required out of them. Crowe and Joaquin are unbelievable of course but it’s those classically trained legends like Richard Harris and Oliver Reed that will have you ready to actually smash through the colosseum. Hans Zimmer was sort of already off and running with Ridley Scott by this point but this sort of kicks off one of the crazier 10-15 year runs that’s up there with John Williams peak.

First time seeing this since Gladiator 2 and I’ll say they do set up quite a bit of details about Lucius, Maximus and Lucilla in the script that I didn’t really remember. There is more continuity that I previously thought. That said, on rewatch, you only see the stark difference in emotional connectivity, production design and scene composition between the two films.
item.dc:creator: Jacob H

item.title: Sentimental Value, 2025 - ★★★★½
item.description:
Really emotionally piercing in such a consistently beautiful way. As a sibling, the complex relationship dynamics felt scarily accurate and it’s really cool that nearly every major performance in this film was nominated for an Oscar. Really washes over you in a way that’s hard to articulate, I thought the music was also outstanding.

2025 Ranked
item.dc:creator: Jacob H

item.title: Vera Cruz, 1954 - ★★★½
item.description:
A really well dressed western that I mostly admired from a distance. The Technicolor is sharp and the blocking is ridiculous. Watching Gary Cooper and Burt Lancaster circle each other has its own appeal, with Lancaster grinning like a shark the entire time.

There’s something slightly off about it in a good way. This came out when the studio western machine was still churning out straight adventure and heroics, but the movie has a cynical streak running through it. It just hides it under all the spectacle and pageantry. The middle stretch drifts a bit and there is honestly way more dancing than expected. Once the last act hits the pessimism finally sharpens and the whole thing ends resoundingly. Solid craftsmanship top to bottom and another from Robert Aldrich checked off.
item.dc:creator: Jacob H

item.title: In Time, 2011 - ★★★
item.description:
Been a day one defender of this. Still super ridiculous, not wrong in its depiction to condemn social financial structure however hamfisted the script is, and is shot by Roger Deakins.

I swear they say “Time” so often and emphasize the hell out of it, they might as well look at the camera when they say it. Trying to ride the social network wave with Timerberlake and it would be better with pretty much any other lead from 2011. Imagine a Michael Fassbender or Clive Owen, goodness what a film you’d have. Either way, I have determined that this movie is good and cool. Cillian Murphy’s Time Crisis 2.
item.dc:creator: Jacob H

item.title: The Haunting, 1999
item.description: Watched on Sunday March 15, 2026.
item.dc:creator: Jacob H

item.title: Ocean's Thirteen, 2007 - ★★★
item.description: Watched on Saturday March 14, 2026.
item.dc:creator: Jacob H

item.title: Fireworks, 1997 - ★★★★★
item.description:
Watching Takeshi Kitano’s films strictly in order of release has been a strange experience because it becomes almost impossible to describe exactly why they work yet he’s already become one of my favorite directors of all time. The mechanics are simple enough on paper. Long silences, sudden violence, characters who barely articulate anything they are feeling. But the effect is something else entirely. It feels like a magic act built out of meditation, regret and irredeemability.

What makes Hana-bi so fascinating in that progression is that it might actually be the most emotionally direct thing he had made up to this point. Oddly enough it felt less experimental to me than something like Sonatine. That might just be the benefit of watching these films chronologically. By now you have already learned Kitano’s rhythms. The strange pockets of humor, the way entire emotional states exist between lines of dialogue. If someone dropped into Hana bi cold I could easily see it feeling sudden or surreal, but after Violent Cop and Boiling Point it almost reads like refinement. The same language, just turned toward quantifiable loss instead of existential drift.

And loss is really what this film is built around. Earlier Kitano films often treat violence like a blunt interruption. Something that erupts without warning and then disappears just as quickly. Here the violence isn’t something casual that happens but instead the only thing you can’t get over. The inciting tragedy keeps returning in slow motion, almost like intrusive memories, fracturing the narrative into pieces that feel closer to mourning than plot. Then there are the paintings shown frequently. Out of nowhere the film opens up into these bursts of hand drawn artwork that feel completely detached from the crime story but emotionally essential to it. They almost function like the interior life of the film. Something the characters themselves are incapable of expressing.

By this point in Kitano’s filmography, watching everything linearly, it is hard not to see his style as this strange balancing act between detachment and overwhelming emotion. The characters barely speak. Entire scenes pass in silence. But the feeling underneath it all keeps building until it is impossible to ignore. The ending on the beach is every emotion at once and very similar to nearly all his other endings as his films seem to keep getting more emotional rather than strictly existential which is interesting. A life wasted, a new one beginning. It is all the same and something as small as a brief embrace can be all there ever was.

My only real gripe is the Joe Hisaishi score not being used much more. It’s so good every time and I felt other films emphasized it more, this would be my #1b with Sonatine being #1a. Both masterpieces and I’m honestly ready to put Kids Return in there too because I still think about that film constantly.

Takeshi Kitano Ranked
item.dc:creator: Jacob H

item.title: Bullitt, 1968 - ★★★★
item.description:
To say Bullitt greatly influenced every crime movie from 1968 onward would be a massive understatement. Its fingerprints are everywhere. The quiet procedural realism, the patience, the way tension builds through work rather than speeches. You can practically draw a straight line from this to The French Connection and Dirty Harry in the immediate aftermath, then watch the DNA keep mutating through things like Thief and To Live and Die in L.A., eventually widening into the procedural sprawl of Heat and L.A. Confidential. Even decades later you can still feel it sitting quietly in the bones of movies like Zodiac and Drive.

The score from Lalo Schifrin shows up sparingly but it sticks in your head immediately. Most of the movie strips the music away entirely and lets the natural sound do the work. Phones ringing, footsteps down hallways, engines idling. That quiet baseline gives the whole film a gritty realism that never feels pushed. At a certain point even a fax machine somehow becomes a source of elite suspense. It’s incredibly barebones and refined. For long stretches you’re basically just watching a man do his job until it goes wrong. Dialogue is minimal, scenes play out patiently, and tension constantly leaks out of everyday police work. It’s before real procedurals were being highlighted but this is right on the cusp so you see the outline in a way that’s really cool. That approach also works perfectly for Steve McQueen, whose naturally removed screen presence fits the movie like a glove. Because of that restraint, when the famous car chase finally kicks in it doesn’t arrive in the form of some huge set piece. It just starts happening and for a movie that spends most of its time in silence or quietly observing it suddenly becomes the loudest thing you’ll hear. Seeing that green 68 Mustang GT literally tear through San Francisco is some of the most badass shit you’ll ever see. The machine is probably one of the most recognizable characters from the late 60s and early 70s. Still quite easily on the Mount Rushmore of car chases in film.
item.dc:creator: Jacob H

item.title: Priest, 2011 - ★★½
item.description: Scott Stewart made Legion, Priest and Dark Skies from 2010 to 2013 and dipped. FX Legend.
item.dc:creator: Jacob H

item.title: Good Luck, Have Fun, Don't Die, 2025 - ★★
item.letterboxd:filmTitle: Good Luck, Have Fun, Don't Die
item.description: Just way messier and not as good as a ton of other stuff doing the same as of recent. This really gets stuck spinning the wheels for me and the first hour is an uphill battle then turns into like Y2K from a couple years ago. There isn’t so much a structure problem as there is a pacing issue. For something trying to capture a particular energy it never quite finds its rhythm. The whole thing ends up sitting in this strange zone where the provocation just constantly flounders. A second act reveal involving Juno Temple plays like a big swing but comes across pretty hacky not because of her ever. You can tell they are trying to shock with a particular real world issue being handled so flippantly, and maybe if the whole movie was not a cornball contest it would not make me so sour. It doesn’t even settle on this and sort of runs off in every direction. It also suffers from coming out at a time when numerous films very recently have done this in a more succinct, hard bitten and severely comedic manner. Meanwhile Sam Rockwell is doing his absolute best to carry some real nonsense on his back. The second act of this movie genuinely feels like it lasts 90 minutes. Given the title and nature it is not the most surprising but it really ends up sitting in a kind of nothing zone for me.
item.dc:creator: Jacob H

item.title: Tenet, 2020 - ★★★★
item.description:
Nolan’s been married to Mann his whole career. The Dark Knight was him sneaking Heat into a superhero movie, but Tenet is his little Blackhat-Miami Vice baby, wind turbines, freeports, yachts, professionals moving like clockwork through deadly infrastructure with time inversion on top. On top of every other world detachment homage of the king, he names Robert Pattinson Neil, arms him like a pro, and the second he steps on screen it’s obvious Nolan should be the first to honor producing Heat 2, he’d be able to pull whatever budget necessary and it would just be too much of a perfect full-circle moment for both filmmakers who clearly have been speaking to each other, and more importantly me, for decades.

It’s exciting when committing to pushing technical limits of filmmaking, even when it comes at a detriment to things like narrative congruence or digestibility among others.
item.dc:creator: Jacob H

item.title: Transformers: Revenge of the Fallen, 2009 - ★★
item.description:
Michael Bay wrote this on Burger King napkins during the writers strike without oversight and naturally made the most racist and sexist Transformers movie imaginable.

They reintroduce John Turturro at exactly 1 hour and 15 minutes which is where the movie really starts. Such a mess it almost feels like it’s unlocking a new form in real time, I think I officially like it more than Age of Extinction simply cause the runtime is 30 minutes shorter but it’s so bad. There is something about them just winging it while filming on the actual Egyptian pyramids that’s so hysterical I love it again.

Michael Bay Ranked
item.dc:creator: Jacob H

item.title: Dead Man's Wire, 2025 - ★★½
item.description:
Couldn’t really care less about what’s going on with any of this and the fairly cheap Sidney Lumet imitation. They at least had the wherewithal to pay homage by casting a borderline senile Al Pacino who’s complaining about a burrito not being cut into thirds within 5 minutes. I was like Lucille Bluth every time she sees Gene Parmesan whenever he was on screen twice. I mean no surprise people are grilling him but I thought it was by far the most entertaining point of the movie. There is some real ‘True Story’ staleness that sands any possible tension.

70s set dressing looks good enough but something always felt off to me. Like it’s wearing another’s skin. The still photo inserts don’t mesh with the acting for me, one takes me out of the other and I’m not sure which is guilty. I may want to have a chat with Colman Domingo’s agent because why is this man getting 19 minutes of screen time when he should be getting infinity minutes of screen time? Answer me this.

2026 Ranked
item.dc:creator: Jacob H

item.title: Sirāt, 2025 - ★★★★½
item.description:
Doubled back before work. Inject the vibes into my veins. Redemption is really just a road that’s a million miles long on 1 millimeter thick glass. Seeing nearly a whole theater sweat in anxiety before feeling completely manipulated and stunned for the rest of their Sunday brings me unexplainable levels of joy. People flabbergasted and even more so when I clapped hard at the end. Yeah hate to break it to you but it’s not exactly Stranger Things Season 5. Bunch of goofs, plugging their ears and closing their eyes. Give it all to me and keep shooting stuff in 16mm, it feels so real.

Even more infatuated after hearing Oliver Laxe talk extensively about his multiple years journey to create this. He talks so freely about what it means to try and create, why you do it and the complexities behind personal expression and providing to the masses. Also his unmistakable stance that the world is in collective pain, that can’t really be compared or comforted supremely by one another but things like cinema of a specific notion can begin a slow healing collective. I think it’s special and really deepened the pretty sparse dialogue which does quite clearly layout most of his sincere intensity.

“Do you think this is what the end of the world is?”
“I don’t know. It’s been the end of the world for a long time now”

It’s these ideas that allow me to take the leap beyond this extreme despair in order to find any sort of answer or solace. I think there is immense respect shown to this odd sublet of rave culture when it didn’t necessarily need to be. It’s an intense belief that’s never played for laughs but it’s not really any cause to. My highest endorsement.
item.dc:creator: Jacob H

item.title: War Machine, 2026 - ★★
item.description:
The sad thing about me I’m going to watch the straight up US Army Call of Duty propaganda funded by whatever streaming feels like pulling up the Brinks truck, pretty much every time. It’s a deep sickness of mine and I know it. They actually hit a bait and switch with it becoming a Syfy original. Visual effects sort of on par with its capability but surprisingly gory which is cool. The most cannonball ham ever and still somehow leaps and bounds better than the Rebel Moon fiascos, not saying anything. It’s certainly not Land of Bad I’ll tell you.

Ritchson is fine, fighting against some real cringe and generics. Can’t wait for him to hard zag one of these days. Movie is like the live action version of Mr. Incredible having to fight that update robot that Syndrome made.
item.dc:creator: Jacob H

item.title: Sirāt, 2025 - ★★★★
item.description: Buddy, you had me a desert rave turns into satan’s playground. They know from second one, that the sound design and music better be on point. It’s exceptional. A film that pulsates through you and cares not about what you’re able to parce out of the madness. How movies should be, strange and forceful without a real desire to explain. The title carries its own weight. The Sirāt is the bridge souls must cross on Judgment Day, suspended over hell and said to be thinner than a hair. The film moves with that same fragile tension, people drifting forward through dust and silence like they’re already halfway across it. This had me sitting up and changing positions multiple times, remarkably tense to say the least. GOATED soundtrack. Tactile and incredibly gritty. Some of the vastness and lingerings on the desert really do a balance act with total failure and hopelessness. Amazing time, made me think of Climax. Really my bag.
item.dc:creator: Jacob H

item.title: Boyz n the Hood, 1991 - ★★★★★
item.description:
Unbelievable stuff, The kind that changes your entire core. So beautiful and honest but it’s like trying to swallow marbles for the whole runtime. Inarguably one of the best feature length debuts in the history of movie making. Makes generations of pain so vividly come through the screen, it feels like you can touch them. There are 2-3 times where characters give each other a quick embrace and it’s some of the most authentic releases on tension you’ll find.

Laurence Fishburne not getting a supporting nomination in 92 for Furious Styles is a little egregious to me. I love Michael Lerner specifically in Barton Fink but c’mon now. Haven’t seen Bugsy but they felt it earned 3 acting nominations (1 leading, 2 supporting) so no doubt I’ll be watching Bugsy reluctantly this year just to see the idea.
item.dc:creator: Jacob H

item.title: Safe, 2012 - ★★★
item.description: Hell yeah. Really strong choreography, editing only holds it slightly back.
item.dc:creator: Jacob H

item.title: The Fall, 2006 - ★★★½
item.description: Watched on Wednesday March 4, 2026.
item.dc:creator: Jacob H

item.title: The Guilty, 2021 - ★★½
item.description:
Gyllenhaal not be nominated for Nightcrawler is such a joke. You could argue he should have won too. I don’t blame him for pivoting away from things like Enemy, Prisoners or Nightcrawler since he seemed to be completely giving himself over and no one cared. I too would then start doing things like Southpaw, Spider-Man, Ambulance or even The Guilty. Regardless of movie quality, you’ll only ever see him elevating things or just making consistently interesting choices when it comes to acting. You get even a slightly less capable actor or director is when you get something like War of the Worlds with Ice Cube.

Fuqua has always known that if you’re going to stick to classic conventions, it’s best if you make it a little mean and spiteful. My guy for sure but I’m really nervous about this Michael Jackson biopic that’s been in the editing room for 7 years. From the man who brought you Training Day and Shooter, presents…..Michael.

Antoine Fuqua Ranked
item.dc:creator: Jacob H

item.title: There Will Be Blood, 2007 - ★★★★★
item.description:
When this came out it was easier to treat Daniel Plainview like a historical irregularity. A grotesque relic from some oil soaked frontier, a man so singular in his cruelty that you could file him away as a piece of foundational America and keep him safely in the past.

Nearly twenty years later he feels like just about every human in a position of power instead of this aberration. Now all the Daniel Plainviews are still waiting to slit the throats of everyone in their sleep. I only love this film more over time but I think how it changes is fascinating. I remember once looking at this groveling oil baron like an alien in disguise. Real unfortunate that I feel like I know Plainview personally and it’s because I do. He runs every facet of my life and I have no say in the matter. The movie has not changed, it is still the most absorbing piece you’ll find and spiritually sick to the core. What has changed is the air around it. The naked contempt, the appetite disguised as vision, the way partnership is just acquisition in nicer language, none of it plays exaggerated in the slightest anymore.

Everyone else is just fighting for air inside something they designed. “I’m finished” used to feel like the ending sign-off of a madman. Now it feels like a mission statement that we are all forced to see through to the end.
item.dc:creator: Jacob H

item.title: The Long Good Friday, 1980 - ★★★★
item.description:
Shout out davedeanstl for staying on me about this one. He’s been trying to put me on for a minute and I finally locked in. I just know Guy Ritchie loved this movie, the DNA is everywhere in that swaggering British dialect and violence amongst many other traits.

Always grateful for subtitles with these because they are moving. Hoskins is compact and volatile. He feels like ambition barely contained in a body, with those teeth showing the entire time. That ending is super memorable and the score will be one of my favorites.

Very glad I finally got around to it.
item.dc:creator: Jacob H

item.title: Crime 101, 2026 - ★★★
item.description:
I get it. I too, kneel at the alter of Michael Mann 7/365. This does however feel like someone studied him down to the molecular level but never really quite absorbed what makes him ache.

I am always in the bag for professionalism porn, for men who live by codes and stare out at cities like they are trying to solve themselves, so on paper this is exactly my thing. It has the night gleam, the glass apartments, the sense that Los Angeles is somehow both overcrowded and spiritually vacant. It understands how those movies look. What it never quite finds is the marrow. The opening pulled me in because it felt clean and adult and confident, like it was about to lock into something obsessive, but after that it mostly just coasts on its own polish. I respect the runtime and at the same time I could not believe how many characters feel cut off without any real sense of completion even at 2 hours 20 minutes. I don’t even think having 3 hours at their disposal would have fixed this.. The movie does not build toward an inevitable collision so much as it eventually runs out of road and boy does it by the end. The cast is almost too stacked, which weirdly flattens the whole thing. Everyone is solid, everyone is committed, but they are all operating at the same muted frequency, lots of hushed and guttural mumbling like seriousness is something you achieve by lowering your voice. Nick Nolte at least sounds like his lungs are full of gravel, which gives the movie a texture it desperately needs.

It clearly wants that Heat level framework and duality, that sense of two lives defined by code circling each other in the dark, but Mann’s movies work because the professionalism is tied to obsession and loneliness and a kind of spiritual inadequacy that’s being self imposed yet unable to be explained. There frankly just isn’t even time spent in the obscure for things to stick longer. Here it’s more like a surface study. You get the poses and the night air and the empty stares, but not the sickness underneath that makes those images mean something. Lacks a desperation and urgency pretty much after the first act and it carries to the end.

I do not want to be completely dismissive because it is watchable and well made and never embarrassing. It just lives on that Triple Frontier tier of competence where you admire the craft in the moment and then realize an hour later that nothing actually stuck to you. Maybe I should rewatch the first Den of Thieves because say what you want, that movie is at least having fun. It wants to stand on the same level as the movies it is borrowing from, and it never quite convinces you it belongs there.
item.dc:creator: Jacob H

item.title: 99 River Street, 1953 - ★★★★½
item.description: Torn for hours on whether to give this 5 stars or not. Perfectly aligns with a mood I frequently seek out and accomplishes more in 83 minutes than most modern stories would require well over two hours to do.

item.title: Marvin’s Favorites
item.description:
My cat Marvin will watch movies with me sometimes depending how interesting he finds them. I’ve been trying to figure out what he likes but it seems almost completely random. Here is my ranking of everything he’s watched according to what he tells me. If you want me to show anything to Marvin then leave it in the comments!
